segments.text: And we'd love to be able to have questions for you within that.
Absolutely. So just by way of introduction, my name
is Sarah Wilson, and I'm the General Manager of Advocacy at New Zealand
Winegrowers. For those who may not be familiar with us, we're the peak
industry body representing New Zealand's 1,400 grape growers and
winemakers. I'll just very briefly speak to a couple of the points in our
submission. I won't restate the whole submission.
New Zealand Winegrowers is supportive of the passage of modern slavery legislation
in New Zealand.
Our industry does not tolerate worker exploitation, and we support our
members to meet their domestic and international obligations in this regard.
As part of this, we publish guidance to assist businesses meet their, to meet those
obligations, such as a voluntary code of practice for our people and through our
sustainability program, Sustainable Winegrowing New Zealand.
What we're seeing through our experience of operating that program is an increasing
trend of market gatekeepers and non-governmental organizations
requiring additional sustainability measures, particularly those
related to people, workforce, and social aspects.
Today, I would just like to briefly summarize two of the points
of particular relevance to our sector.
The first, that any overseas reporting requirements already in place should
be recognized and be allowed to be used to reduce duplication,
such as by the use of equivalent statements.
If requirements are overly prescriptive and do not account for good work that's
already being done, there's a risk that it will add cost without necessarily
improving outcomes.
Secondly, while the legislation is of course focused on reporting entities, it's
important to consider the impacts that may occur throughout the supply chain,
including to small businesses, and it will be important to ensure that any flow on
impacts to those businesses are not unduly burdensome.
We believe that education has an important role to play here to help all those
businesses come up to speed with any additional requirements from this
legislation, and such an education sector approach should not be
solely focused on reporting entities that are, or may be impacted in
some way.
Thank you again for the opportunity to submit, and I'm very happy to take any
questions that you may have.
Hmm. I'll use my prerogative, Sarah, just
to chip in with one starting question.
Do you know if any of your members are currently reporting in
other international markets?
Yes, absolutely. We do have a number of
businesses meeting a variety of different requirements, if I can put it that
way. So,
for example, some of those international gatekeepers are increasingly
imposing, uh, im-imposing various
requirements in this area, but there's a lack of consistency internationally.
So that fragmentation is-
Hmm
... creating, often quite a lot of, compliance
administration, if I can put it that way.
But, but yes, certainly a number of our members are already engaging with these
types of requirements in the international space.
Hmm. In the, in the likes of Australia, the UK, Europe?
Yes, absolutely. They're all significant export markets for our members.
Right. Okay. Thank you. Greg, you look like you're champing at the bit, my friend.
Yeah, just picking up on that question there, Phil.
What, uh, have you got some examples of, of the, of
unhelpful complexities or burdens that are coming through on, on the, on
the, some of the reporting that, some of the members are, are already having to,
having to perform?
One example is, is, is, just that there are not, that the
requirements are not harmonized.
So you've got requirements coming from retailers or importers, you've got
requirements coming from, from governments, and then you've got requirements
from, in, in some cases, for example, in the Nordic countries, their
liquor monopolies. So specifically in the alcohol space there are monopoly
retailers, and so they kind of sit in that in-between space between
government and private sector, and they will set, effectively,
market access requirements. So it's just that divergence that in and of itself is a
challenge.
More specifically, what we've been engaging with through our sustainability program
is around,
increasingly, countries that don't recognize a risk-based approach or
recognize a local context. So, for example, one of the,
the Nordic sustainability programs we've been working with at the moment, they
no longer account for existing protective measures and local
legislation. From their perspective, they consider those to be not relevant
and require those to be duplicated by a sustainability scheme.
Hmm.
Have you, any thoughts on the current threshold or the proposed
threshold of $100 million? Do you think that's about right, or would you want to
see that adjusted?
We don't have any specific comments on the threshold.
If it was to be materially changed, then that's something I think we'd, we'd hope
would be consulted on again if there was a significant difference.
Right. Okay. And then what about director liability?
You'll be aware that under the proposed legislation, there'll be, there's,
individual directors could be exposed to a criminal prosecution.
I don't think we've specifically addressed that point in our submission.
We'd probably defer to the submission of, of Business New Zealand-
Business New Zealand
... on those, those particular topics.
Yeah, great. Okay. Thank you very much.
So Sarah, I'm guessing that
quite a few of your members would be selling into,
companies who are reporting entities in those other markets,
right? So they're gonna be, those entities are gonna be
requiring, some of your members to
satisfy them by, you know, I guess they send them a questionnaire or something like
that saying, "We have to report on, on modern slavery.
Can you give us a report on the, on the products that you're selling to us?" Is
that right?
Yes, absolutely.
Hmm. So it's a good example of how, how a, a,
a regime like this or like what the Australians have, cascades down
through
a lot of suppliers.
Absolutely.
Yeah.
Okay, any other questions, colleagues?
That's great. Thank you.
Okay. Thanks, Sarah. Very helpful.
Thank you all. Take care.
Great.

Next up we've got Ralph Simpson.
Yeah, morning, committee.
Hello, Ralph.
I may be one of the few
members or submitters who've actually worked at the
coalface on human trafficking. So I was working in Asia and Africa for
five years from
2015 to 2019. Also worked for Fletcher
Building for six years, so I know what the constraints on big business
is. And on top of that, I was a partner in a law firm, a major law firm, for
30 years. So I have four points. Modern slavery
laws like this have no measurable impact on
trafficking in persons and nor will they ever.
It's totally unrealistic. I understand that the
international pressure on us to align ourselves
with similar legislation in other countries, but let's be candid with one
another, particularly on international supply chains, this
legislation does not work. That was noted by the MB paper.
And
in one sense, if it was able to work, one would've expected to see
that in the annual reports filed by companies where they can say,
"We found slavery in our supply chain and we stopped it."
And you just do not see that anywhere.
Number two, imposing obligations like this on the business community
is unrealistic and frankly unfair, particularly when the state is so
woefully underperforming as noted in the US
state TIP report.
And third, my point is that we should focus on exploitation
that is going on in New Zealand, as noted in the trafficking in persons report from
the US State Department, and clean up our own backyard.
And that's really more of a government issue than a business issue.
The last thing I want to touch on briefly, and I'll just mention it now,
I suggest clauses 28 and 29 of the bill be amended.
At the moment it says where a company has
breached its obligations, the Crown can make no payments
under the Public Finance Act, and there's no sunset clause on
that. So I think I'd suggest that that prohibition
applies for 24 or say 36 months, but not be completely
open-ended into infinity. So I think that's just a tweak that needs to
be done. So, listen, let's deal with the first point.
Modern slavery laws are ineffective.
Walk Free suggest there's 50 million victims, although
that is largely guesswork. But,
if one looks at, for example, the UNODC biannual report on
trafficking, there's minimum examples of its success.
So, the number of victims detected, that's
people that have actually been identified as trafficking victims
from 2020 to 2023, so four years, was
out of the 50 million.
The number of persons prosecuted, 48,000.
The number of persons convicted, 15 or nearly
16,000. And they are largely foot soldiers, so not
the people in the shadows making the big money, but rather
foot soldiers that are readily replaced.
So you can see from that that even when we're talking about the
UN, EU, law enforcement with resources,
statutory investigative and arrest powers, ability to phone
tap and do all the other things that modern law enforcement agencies can
do, the impact is
minimal. You will not stop international trafficking,
and claims to the contrary are delusionary.
So
let's try and focus on what we can do, and that is domestic
modern slavery activities, particularly in construction,
horticulture, including the grape growers industry,
and also where we've got
in the, what do I call it, the entertainment industry, like
restaurants,
brothels, et cetera, where that sort of thing can go on and where we
can do something about it. And really that's going to require
the government to spend some money.
We need labor inspectors to be interviewing migrant
workers, checking they've still got their official
documents, passports, et cetera, that their accommodation is
appropriate, that they are receiving the wages they were promised.
And there's quite a lot of work to be done in that area.
But to expect companies to do that is utterly impossible.
And I have been working with TF Fund a little, and I respect them and World
Vision, but the suggestion that we should introduce due diligence
is frankly laughable and unrealistic.
They refer me to the UN document on how to undertake
due diligence, and it is just woeful.
It just does not answer the questions.
And when we know that there is modern slavery in the supply chain for
solar panels and for cobalt used in the production
of, and manufacturing of rechargeable lithium
batteries, if you're serious about this, you will stop.
No electronics into the country with lithium batteries.
So that's EVs, hybrids, computers, phones, all have to
stop. Are we seriously saying that? And it's the same with solar panels.
It's notorious that the Muslim minorities in China are being
used to produce the raw ingredients for solar panels.
Are we really saying, bearing in mind, I think,
98% of solar wafers come from China, that we will
stop importing solar panels? You need to be very careful with this
legislation because you're asking companies to do things that you really don't
want them to do.
I can't imagine any
government party or parliamentary party
seriously suggesting that we stop importing lithium
rechargeable batteries or solar panels.
And it's the same with complex things like, if we
just take a power drill, battery-powered power drillHow far do you
want businesses to go back in their auditing of their supply chains?
Are we going all the way back to mining the raw materials that make aluminum and
steel and plastics? These are huge obligations, and the
suggestion that companies somehow are supposed to do
due diligence and stop this modern slavery, and that
directors should be prosecuted when the government is so woefully
behind in dealing with its own domestic issues
is frankly unfair and unrealistic.
Ralph, thank you. I've got to stop you there, unfortunately, because that is a very
challenging and interesting submission, and I wish we had more time to discuss
it. But we will give it serious thought.
So thank you very much.
Thank you.
Yeah.
Okay. Next up, we have, I think, Charlie from Vic
Labour. Charlie, you've got
10 minutes, and we would like to have some time for questions if possible.
Yeah. Kia ora koutou. And thank you, Ralph.
I resonate with much of what you said, especially with those domestic issues.
Yeah. So my name's Charlie, Charlie Kadri. I'm from Vic Labour.
I'm the Māori vice chair.
Vic Labour is the youth branch of the Labour Party here in Te Whanganui-a-Tara, and
we consist of students in Mangatahi.
Yeah, I'd like to thank you guys for the opportunity, special opportunity to speak
on this historic piece of legislation.
I'd like to thank Carmela Belich from Labour and
Greg Fleming from National for bringing this forward in a uniquely bipartisan
approach.
I remember the
last year of my high school in sociology, wondering if this bill would ever come to
fruition. So I'd like to thank you both for your efforts in bringing this bill to
the House.
So Vic Labour widely supports the Modern Slavery Bill, what it intends to do in
being able to identify, address, mitigate, and remediate the
harm and injustices caused by modern slavery that exists within our supply
chains.
There is two points of criticism in this bill, though, that I'd like to bring
forward. So we are concerned that by excluding all businesses below
$100 million of revenue, modern slavery will continue to
persist in our supply chains.
The threshold excludes the vast majority of New Zealand businesses who can be
just as reliant on those supply chains.
And so we would recommend that the Select Committee consider the
consultation with MBI in 2022, which proposes a
staggered bracketing of revenue and responsibilities,
so more proportionate to businesses and
what they can do to remediate modern slavery.
We are also concerned about the decision not to introduce further penalties.
This is because businesses may not be encouraged to improve practices from
only simply providing statements.
They might just begin viewing it as simply box-checking,
another part of business. So while we do appreciate that
transparency is great, we appreciate that, but if
we want to encourage real change, we will need penalties that are ready.
And so kind of two penalties that we sort of go through in our submission that can
be introduced is something like a penalty for high reliance.
So that's being if you go above a certain threshold, you
are deemed high reliant and you're penalized for that.
And then a penalty for continued engagement.
So that can be a reoccurring engagement below another set
penalty in subsequent years.
This wasn't in our written submission, but I would also ask the
committee to consider if these
penalties go far enough.
Maximum fines are 600,000 in New Zealand
in this bill, and so
that is .6% of the $100 million of revenue for those
businesses that need to provide a report.
If we're to look over at New South Wales, we've got a maximum fine of 1.1
million, and those are businesses with $50 million
of revenue, so half that of what we're doing.
And so I think this is an important part to remind the committee
that we are talking about big businesses here, right?
So these are businesses with $100 million exceeding in
revenue. They are by far capable.
They have resources to provide a report and be on their way.
And if they don't provide a report, there's a relatively small fine compared to
their revenue. So we can afford to strengthen our compliance settings
and penalties applied to these big businesses for the moral good
of addressing the injustices of modern slavery.
And not doing so risks undermining the intent of this bill.
So besides these kind of recommendations and considerations, what we hope the
Select Committee take into account and integrate in some way, we
strongly support the bill and view the bill as a historic
step in the right direction. Yeah, happy to take questions.
Thanks, Charlie. Colleagues, any questions?
Greg.
No, that was very, very clear. Thank you very much for both that, our
submission, and also for the
clarity of your written submission as well.
Maybe one, seeing as that you did hear the previous
submitter, do you have any thoughts on essentially what, when you
talk in your submission about your desire
to see companies do more than just report, you're essentially
asking them for a form of due diligence, which is the
thing that Ralph pushed back very hard on based on his
experience,
both working internationally and then also representing one of New
Zealand's largest corporates. Have you got any thoughts on that?
Well, I think that the contents of the modern slavery
statement that's in this bill,
a
lot of it's by interpretation. So there's no,
like-Set way of providing a
report, which I will assume that there will be a bit more guidance once it actually
comes.
Yeah.
But there it's not much.
You simply have to identify where those supply chains are
that engage with modern slavery, and then give that in the statement,
which I can imagine businesses with a hundred millions in revenue can do
like any other report that they've got to give to government.
Yeah. Okay.
Thank you. Thank you again. Thanks very much, Charlie.
I appreciate your passion for this and the amount of time that
you've invested in it.
Thank you for your time. Thank you.
Thanks, Charlie. Good stuff.
Thank you.
Cool.
Okay, next up is Citizens Advice Bureau, Louise
and Gwyneth. Welcome to our subcommittee.
You've got
10 minutes, and we would love to have a chance to have a couple of minutes for
questions within that time.
Great.
Kia ora. All right, just trying to-- I think I can see everything. Yeah.
So my name's Gwyneth Kerry-Smith.
I am the National Data Analyst for Citizen Advice Bureau, New Zealand.
I'm here with my colleague, Louise May, the National Policy Advisor.
So we will take our submission as read and just use this time to
highlight our key points. Firstly, just a brief overview
of the Citizen Advice Bureau.
So we're a national wide, locally based community organization
providing free, confidential and independent information,
advice and support to the public.
We're in 80 locations across the Motu, and our service is
provided by 2,000 trained volunteers.
Our service is for everyone, and we assist with whatever issue a
person brings to us. So over the past year, our CABs have helped
with over 300,000 client inquiries on issues
such as employment, immigration, housing,
relationships, consumer problems and heaps more.
Every time a CAB volunteer assists a person, they enter the
detail of the inquiry and the help they gave them into our
inquiry database. And this information gives us a
rich insight into the issues that are impacting people across our society.
So we use what we learn from our clients to speak to our law,
policies and services that uphold people's rights and strengthen their
wellbeing.
So the CAB is a key source of help for people who have come to New
Zealand for employment, only to find themselves in
exploitative work situations. So several
years ago, we started tracking in our client inquiry data those
cases where migrant worker exploitation or trafficking appears to
be occurring. And we started doing this because CABs
across Aotearoa were telling us that they were seeing more
people in these situations, and that there is a lack of the support
that people need to be able to
easily move out of these vulnerable situations and not experience
further harm.
Over a two-year period, our volunteers have responded to around
1,000 of these inquiries, and so we have analyzed those, and they
provide a base for our submission and our
recommendations for this bill.
So when New Zealanders hear the term modern slavery, they
probably tend to think of something that happens elsewhere, not here in
Aotearoa. Unfortunately, modern slavery
is happening on our shores. It has been for some time,
and it's not just a few outlier cases, it's a systemic problem,
and our client data shows this.
So in analyzing these
1,000 cases, we see situations where people are
working in conditions that appear to meet the definition of modern slavery,
where they're being used and controlled by their employer as if their
employer owns them.
So we have people whose employers make them work
long days, seven days a week, with no time to be with their children,
spouse, family or friends.
Employers who hold their passports,
hold their bank cards and control their money, don't pay them enough to
survive on and steal from their wages.
Who set them up in employer provided accommodation
that is also unhealthy and unsafe.
And they don't let them make phone calls out of earshot, don't let them leave the
workplace for vital appointments, phone them at all hours of the day and
night,
and move them around the country without the worker having any say in the matter.
They also can threaten them with their visa status that will be revoked, that they
will be deported, or their family in their home country will be harmed by
associates or family of the employer.
And that's if they complain, report their situation or try to leave.
So these people are living and working under these conditions here
in New Zealand. They are not free. They are enslaved.
So we know that this problem is systematic because we
see these situations like this around the
country across a range of industries.
For example, hospitality, primary industries,
retail, cleaning, beauty and
personal care industries, and also in the trades.
So we know the problem is underreported because workers who are stepping forward
to get our help often tell us that their coworkers are--
They're usually in New Zealand on work visas as well, they've been subjected to the
same treatment.We also have clients who need our help but do
not want their circumstances recorded in any way for fear
of retribution. So those cases are also not ending up in our
statistics.
Yeah, so you can imagine how difficult it is working under these kind of
conditions that I've just described for a person to find a way out, to
reach out and to get help.
So we recognize that this bill is a major step forward for New
Zealand, which is lagging behind other nations in legislation
for preventing and addressing modern slavery.
And we commend the cross-party cooperation which
helped to bring this bill before Parliament, and we acknowledge the
people and the civil society organizations whose hard mahi
over many years has led to this moment where we now have legislation
before the House. But we believe the bill needs to go
further. We need to go further as a nation if we're to stop
the modern slavery that's happening here in Aotearoa, and if we're to
provide victim survivors on our shores with the support
they need to move out of these vulnerable situations and to be able to
recover and not experience further harm.
So our recommendations are for
removing the business income threshold from the bill.
As you will have noted in our written submission,
many of our clients who are being subjected to modern slavery
conditions are employed by businesses whose incomes do not
meet the threshold. In fact, many of these businesses would come nowhere
near it. And we think that it would seem
incongruous to have a situation where most, if not all
of the businesses who are actually engaging in modern slavery practices here in
Aotearoa do not come under the ambit of New Zealand's modern
slavery legislation.
We point to another measure, which is for New Zealand to
discontinue the bonding of people on temporary work visas to their
employers.
These visas and the extreme power imbalance in the employer's
favor that they establish have created conditions
favorable to modern slavery in our country, and they're
enabling the problem to persist. Ending tied
visas is an essential part of tackling the modern
slavery that's happening here in New Zealand.
The issue of tied visas may be considered outside the scope of this
bill, but we believe it's highly relevant to its
purpose and intent, and we're asking the members of this committee
to advocate to the Minister for Immigration to untie visas from
employers.
Additionally, we recommend that the bill should not
just require businesses to report on incidents of
modern slavery within their operations and supply chains.
If this bill is to be more than a tick box exercise, if it's
to be a driver of meaningful change, it must,
excuse me, also require businesses to act when
they identify potential or actual modern
slavery within their operations and supply chains.
And we note that Australia's modern slavery legislation, while it
doesn't yet mandate action, that this change is
being signaled. And if we don't reflect this
shift in our own legislation when we have this
opportunity, then New Zealand will continue to lag
behind on the international stage when it comes to acting against modern
slavery. One thing we really want to highlight as
well is that the bill must make provisions for
support for victim survivors in New Zealand.
This is an issue our CABs around the country are particularly
concerned about and where they see an urgent need for
change. Our CABs are witnessing the
harm for victim survivors caused by a lack of
support for income, accommodation, healthcare, and
legal assistance. People are often having to
return to situations of harm, either back to the business that
exploited them or they have to leave the country.
And leaving the country can mean returning to a situation that is made
more precarious or unsafe as a result of how things
unfolded for them here in New Zealand.
Supports for victim survivors should be included in the bill
now. Delaying this provision until the three-year
review will mean that people are continuing to be trapped
in harmful situations because they don't have the means to
survive outside of them.
And finally,
we recommend that the bill establishes the role of a
dedicated, independent, anti-slavery commissioner to
identify risks, monitor implementation, provide
practical guidance and support to help victim survivors move out of
situations of harm, and help businesses meet
expectations and standards for preventing and addressing
exploitation.
The role of an independent commissioner, we believe, is essential for
ensuring that monitoring and enforcement is
applied consistently across government and the private sector, and that the
intent of the bill is achieved.
So in conclusion, we welcome this bill as an
important step forward for Aotearoa in eliminating modern
slavery. But for it to be effective in
practice, it must go beyond reporting
alone.
It must require businesses to take action, and it must also
respond to the realities of people who are experiencing
serious exploitation and modern slavery conditions here in this
country.
Thank you, Louise. Thank you, Gwyneth. Appreciate your submissions.
Thank you.
Okay.

as well as being the first Samoan-UN Indigenous Fellow.

always about physical force.
Sometimes it's coercion through dependency and a lack
of real alternatives.
My
submission is based on my LLB Honors research, which

or the RSE scheme,
and the experiences of temporary migrant workers.

and also future opportunities to

conditions, or even report exploitation,
and even if they are technically free to do so.
And I guess one of the things that I had to sit with is that

So when all of these
things are connected, it makes it very
difficult for somebody that
English is their second language and not being able to

So my second point is also that these

They actually are happening. And recent
accommodation inspections, which I had gotten under the Official

found issues still in existence. Issues such as
overcrowding, inadequate facilities, mold, unhealthy
conditions,
being forced to work even if they're sick.
Yeah, some of their living conditions in the RSE worker accommodation.
My final point is that New Zealand has not
ratified or even signed the United Nation Migrant Workers Convention.
That is not even a mention in the bill,
which that's the main international treaty that's
dedicated to protecting migrant workers.
And I think this is important because it recognizes that migrant
workers can face
particular vulnerabilities arising from employer dependency,

cetera. And if we're going to have
a Modern Slavery Bill, I just think it's really
important that it reflects
those realities and provides strong protections for migrant workers,
including
those participating in the migrant scheme.
And I really feel that it's not just--
I think there was a report that I read and some of the
employers had said something like it's just a few
bad sort of pickings in the apple or whatever,
but I feel like it's the structural system.
The system itself needs to
be changed.
But yeah, that's kind of the points that I wanted to
highlight, and I'm so sorry I had to run up the stairs.
I just finished my very last exam.
So yeah, I didn't really have time to--

I think we have time for one question,
colleagues.

quite detailed responses

but

of,

of any questions. But yeah. It's a
definitely
severe situation, I think.

as mentioned by CAB that
